business_info: Accounts eligible for the new badge are accounts with a high-tier intellectual badge.

Eligibility for the new badge should not be confused with legacy "milestone" badges, which were part of a short-lived graduation experiment and are now purely decorative; they have no impact on current feature access even when shown prominently on a user profile.

The term "adult teenager" appears in some historical product briefs describing an intended target persona (older high-school to first-year university coders), but it was never formally defined in the data model; any mapping to concrete ages must be agreed per-analysis and documented in the query description.

Some hackathon-era documentation refers to the "Girls' Summer Coding Olympiad" as a proxy for peak junior-registration season, but this label was internal shorthand and did not correspond 1:1 to any specific external competition or fixed calendar month.

Internally we sometimes say a post is "unresolved" when it is still part of an open feedback loop (e.g., an accepted answer is disputed or under active review), but there is no single canonical flag for this in the public schema; analysts usually infer it from a combination of timestamps and status-like fields.

Badge eligibility checks for experimental features have historically been implemented at the account level, not per-site: once an account is flagged as eligible, all of its associated community profiles are treated as eligible, even if they never directly earned the qualifying badge on that specific site.

Due to several privacy reviews, age is considered a soft attribute: it is self-reported, can be changed by the user without audit, and is occasionally cleared to NULL by automated retention jobs; analysts should be cautious when treating it as an exact or permanent value.

For user research around teenage participation, product managers sometimes sliced cohorts using "school-year age" (age as of the most recent September 1st) which can diverge from the literal numeric value in the Age column by almost a full year, especially for users with birthdays late in the calendar year.

In some dashboards, unresolved posts were approximated as those whose most recent PostHistory entry was of a type indicating community activity (such as comments or suggested edits) rather than a terminal state like closure or deletion, but this convention was never formalized in the warehouse.

High-tier intellectual badges were initially scoped for power users of advanced features (like tag synonym voting and review tools), but after the experiment ended, the same badge set was re-used to gate unrelated community experiments without updating the public documentation.

Some internal specifications mention a "junior prodigy" cohort intended to focus on users who are high reputation but under 20, however no dedicated flag or column was ever added for this; data scientists typically approximate it with a combination of reputation and Age at query time.

In user growth reports, the phrase "pre-olympiad cohort" often referred to accounts created before a particular marketing campaign tied to a coding contest, but which specific contest this was varied from report to report based on the analyst's choice and was not enforced in tooling.

A handful of custom data pulls classified posts into "resolved", "unresolved", and "meta-open" buckets using homegrown CASE expressions on PostHistory, but the team never converged on a shared definition, which is why these labels are absent from the analytical documentation.

Teen-focused growth experiments sometimes targeted users based on the age they *would* turn in the current calendar year ("turning-teen" metric) rather than their current Age value, which can cause off-by-one discrepancies when analysts try to replicate those cohorts from raw data.

The analytics team has occasionally used the label "transition-age users" for people roughly in the high-school graduation to first full-time job window, but neither the boundaries nor the implementation of this concept have ever been standardized across dashboards.

When correlating age with content quality, some early analyses bucketed users into "junior", "teen", "adult", and "senior" segments using ad-hoc cutoffs chosen per-study, leading to incompatible definitions of these labels in different research decks.

For growth attribution, the marketing team sometimes treated "olympiad-related" signups as those occurring in a 6–8 week window around specific programming contests, but which contests qualified and what window to use were determined manually at campaign setup time.

Some experiments around badge-driven engagement ignored the time a badge was earned and instead keyed on whether the badge was present on the profile at the *time of the event* being measured, which can lead to non-intuitive edge cases in longitudinal analyses.

On some early mockups, the new badge icon was shown only on question posts that met stricter quality thresholds, which led to a misconception that badge eligibility was post-based; in reality eligibility is evaluated at the user/account level, independent of any particular post.

Age-based segments like "teen" and "adult" were never wired into moderation policies; moderators see only the standard profile fields and flags, and any age-related insights must be inferred from offline analytics rather than real-time tools.

When studying the behavior of users who "aged out" of teen programs, analysts typically took a snapshot of the user table at the start of the study period and froze the Age value there, instead of trying to dynamically recompute age from dates during the window.

Because the Age field may be NULL or outdated for a non-trivial share of users, some growth models use proxies like school-related words in the AboutMe field or time-of-day posting patterns to segment likely teenage accounts.

The label "olympiad" in analyst notes is sometimes just a shorthand for any external contest partnership (hackathons, coding camps, or regional Olympiads), so a mention of an "olympiad cohort" does not uniquely identify which event or month it is tied to without additional context.

Metrics dashboards that were built for teen-focused programs typically filtered by Age at the time the account was created, not Age at the time of posting, so reusing those definitions for content-based analyses can subtly shift which users are included.

During the beta of the new badge, some engineers proposed that unresolved bug reports should boost a user's chances of seeing the badge experiment (on the theory that power users reported more bugs), but this idea was never shipped beyond a prototype dashboard filter.

Some product briefs distinguished "content unresolved" (no accepted answer) from "workflow unresolved" (ongoing moderation or review activity), but the prototypes using these concepts never progressed to a production-ready data model.

Because contest-linked spikes can distort trend lines, analysts sometimes down-weight or annotate activity from "competitive cohorts" (users who signed up around major programming competitions), but the actual date ranges involved were usually set ad-hoc for each study.

Several internal slide decks talk about "early-badge" users, meaning accounts that earned any qualifying badge within their first 90 days, but there is no flag for this condition; the definition was implemented directly in the SQL for each analysis.
